answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: Net Zero Revolution: Emissions are in line with the Paris Agreement climate goal of limiting global warming to 1.5°C by the end of the century, with low overshoots (<0.1°C) of 1.5°C in earlier years. This leads to high transition risks, but lower physical climate risks. Adaptive Evolution: There is a lack of international coordination on the climate response, with regions operating independently. Emissions decline but lead nonetheless to 2.7°C of warming by 2100, associated with moderate to severe physical risks. Transition risks are low-moderate. Hot House: Globally, only currently implemented policies are preserved. Emissions increase until 2100, leading to 3°C+ of warming by 2100 and severe physical risks but lower transition risks.
answers.notes: These are Meridian's own named scenarios, built on NGFS, IPCC, CCC and NIWA source data.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: 1.5°C (Net Zero Revolution, with low overshoots <0.1°C of 1.5°C in earlier years); ~2.7°C (Adaptive Evolution); and 3°C+ (Hot House). Temperature overshoot is explicitly mentioned for the 1.5°C pathway.
answers.evidence: Net Zero Revolution: "Emissions are in line with the Paris Agreement climate goal of limiting global warming to 1.5°C by the end of the century, with low overshoots (<0.1°C) of 1.5°C in earlier years." Adaptive Evolution: "Emissions decline but lead nonetheless to 2.7°C of warming by 2100." Hot House: "Emissions increase until 2100, leading to 3°C+ of warming by 2100 and severe physical risks."
answers.location_hint: p.11 and p.13 (scenario overview); p.15–17 (individual scenario pages)
answers.notes: Temperature overshoot (<0.1°C) is explicitly mentioned for the Net Zero Revolution scenario.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple scenario providers are explicitly named: NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System), IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change), CCC (New Zealand Climate Change Commission), and NIWA (National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research).
answers.evidence: "We have based our climate scenarios on the internationally recognised NGFS and IPCC scenarios. We have also incorporated data from reputable domestic scenarios: the Climate Change Commission scenarios, and NIWA physical climate data downscaled for New Zealand (based on the IPCC's fifth assessment RCP scenarios)."
answers.notes: All four providers are explicitly named. Meridian's own WMO (Wholesale Market Outlook) models are also used for energy pathways.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: Net Zero Revolution data sources: "Global – to 2100 – IPCC Shared Socioeconomic Pathway SSP1-1.9. New Zealand – to 2100 – NIWA Representative Concentration Pathway RCP2.6*." Adaptive Evolution: "Global – to 2100 – IPCC SSP2-4.5. NZ – to 2100 – NIWA RCP4.5*." Hot House: "Global – to 2100 – IPCC SSP3-7.0. NZ – to 2100 – NIWA RCP8.5*." The footnote explains: "In FY25, NIWA SSP1-2.6 will be used (published July 2024 – not available in time for FY24 reporting). RCP2.6 has been used, and SSP1-2.6 will be used next year, as these are the lowest emissions scenarios that NIWA has downscaled for New Zealand. NIWA has not downscaled data for RCP1.9 or SSP1-1.9."
answers.notes: The document explicitly uses both SSP identifiers (for global projections) and RCP identifiers (for NZ-downscaled NIWA data) within the same scenarios. The footnote acknowledges this mixing and explains it is due to NIWA not yet having downscaled SSP data at time of reporting.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: "We have also incorporated data from reputable domestic scenarios: the Climate Change Commission scenarios, and NIWA physical climate data downscaled for New Zealand (based on the IPCC's fifth assessment RCP scenarios)." And: "Meridian was able to complement this more widely accessible work with additional bespoke work it had already completed, to help it understand the possible hydro-catchment-specific impacts of climate change." Additionally, Meridian uses its own in-house WMO (Wholesale Market Outlook) models (WMO Revo, WMO Evo, WMO Devo) to model energy pathways within each scenario.
answers.location_hint: p.12–14 (Relevance of scenarios and scenario methodology)
answers.notes: Scenarios are customised in multiple ways: (1) NIWA data is downscaled for New Zealand; (2) Meridian conducted bespoke hydro-catchment-specific analysis; (3) Meridian's own WMO models are used for energy pathways; (4) a STEEP analysis was conducted to tailor driving forces to Meridian's specific business context.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: "A group of relevant Meridian personnel was appointed to review and update the scenarios ahead of FY24 disclosure. The team included personnel from our modelling team, Sustainability and Strategy and Risk functions. An externally contracted climate disclosures specialist was also involved in reviewing and updating the scenarios. No other external stakeholders were involved in updating the scenarios in 2024."
answers.notes: The document explicitly states "No other external stakeholders were involved in updating the scenarios in 2024." The original 2023 scenarios were developed with independent peer review from a climate scientist, but no participatory or co-produced process is described.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.evidence: The document describes three distinct climate scenarios (Net Zero Revolution, Adaptive Evolution, Hot House) used to assess risks and opportunities. No explicit baseline or reference scenario against which the three climate scenarios are compared is described. The document does reference a "Current Policies" scenario from NGFS and CCC as the basis for the Hot House scenario, but this is one of the three scenarios rather than a separate baseline.
answers.notes: There is no explicit "business as usual" or "no climate change" baseline scenario described. The Hot House scenario (aligned with NGFS Current Policies) could be interpreted as a de facto baseline, but it is not labelled as such in the document.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: "Meridian's Revolution model (WMO Revo) – which models a low-carbon future, with strong uptake of disruptive technology." "Meridian's Evolution model (WMO Evo) – which models a more business-as-usual mode for the New Zealand electricity system." "Meridian's Devolution Model (WMO Devo) – which models a disrupted and divergent future with limited decarbonisation, de-industrialisation, and difficult conditions for new builds."
answers.notes: These are Meridian's own in-house Wholesale Market Outlook (WMO) models. No external IAMs (e.g. REMIND, MESSAGE, GCAM) or climate models are named directly; the document relies on NGFS, IPCC, CCC and NIWA scenario outputs rather than running those models directly.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: "PR 3 – Increased severe weather events could damage assets and infrastructure. Damage to assets and infrastructure due to severe weather events (storms), resulting in financial loss via reduced generation and increased insurance premiums... Actual financial impact: $5M in carryover costs from the 2023 Cyclone Gabrielle/Hale events in FY24." Also: "PR 1 – More intense, extreme rainfall events impact hydro catchment flood risk. More intense, extreme rainfall events in the hydro catchments and uncertainty of climate change impacts surrounding probable maximum flood levels could affect Meridian's flood flow capacity, potentially resulting in physical damage to spillway/control structures."
answers.notes: The document explicitly includes damage/loss estimates from extreme events (acute physical risks), including a quantified $5M actual financial impact from Cyclone Gabrielle/Hale events. Anticipated financial impacts for future acute events are noted as "not quantified" due to uncertainty.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: "In 2023, Meridian began by identifying the list of transitional and physical variables captured in the context of its unique business – for example, changes in the frequency/intensity of storm events, precipitation, carbon pricing and policy intervention levels in New Zealand and abroad." Scenario driving forces table includes: consumer preferences, availability of skilled workers, peak demand, supply chains, insurance, wholesale electricity prices, increasing temperatures and hot days, extreme rainfall and storm events, dry years and drought, seasonality, New Zealand policy and regulation changes, consenting process, policy incentives for decarbonisation. Physical scenario descriptions also reference sea-level rise and hydro inflows.
answers.location_hint: p.13 (scenario development), p.47–48 (Appendix A driving forces table)
answers.notes: A wide range of non-temperature variables are used across both physical and transition dimensions of the scenarios.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: "We have based our climate scenarios on the internationally recognised NGFS and IPCC scenarios. We have also incorporated data from reputable domestic scenarios: the Climate Change Commission scenarios, and NIWA physical climate data downscaled for New Zealand (based on the IPCC's fifth assessment RCP scenarios)." Each scenario's data sources explicitly distinguish between global (NGFS, IPCC SSPs to 2100) and New Zealand-level (CCC to 2050, NIWA RCPs downscaled for NZ to 2100) data. Meridian also conducted "additional bespoke work it had already completed, to help it understand the possible hydro-catchment-specific impacts of climate change."
answers.location_hint: p.12–17 (Relevance of scenarios and individual scenario data sources)
answers.notes: Global models (NGFS/IPCC) are used for global projections, while NIWA data is explicitly described as "downscaled for New Zealand." Hydro-catchment-specific analysis adds a further local dimension.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short term: today to 2030; Medium term: 2030 to 2050; Long term: 2050 to 2100. Rationale is partially provided: longer time horizons are justified by asset useful lives and the nature of physical climate risks increasing over time. Business planning horizons (short 1–5 years, medium 5–10 years, long 10–30 years) are also noted as distinct from the climate scenario horizons.
answers.evidence: "Meridian's annual climate risk and opportunity assessment considers scenarios across three time horizons. Longer time horizons are helpful in considering the useful lives of assets (beyond a typical business case horizons). These horizons will continue to be reviewed based on emerging and relevant contexts, including climate science. The time horizons are as follows: Short term: from today through to 2030. Medium term: from 2030 to 2050. Long term: from 2050 to 2100." Also: "Meridian's business planning and capital allocation timeframes are currently defined as: short 1–5 years; medium 5–10 years; and long term 10–30 years."
answers.location_hint: p.5 (Risk Management, Time horizons) and p.11 (Strategy, Planning horizons)
answers.notes: The rationale for longer time horizons (asset useful lives, increasing physical risk uncertainty) is briefly stated. The document also notes that climate scenarios extend to 2100 to capture physical risks, while business planning typically uses a 30-year horizon.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: "In 2023, Meridian began by identifying the list of transitional and physical variables captured in the context of its unique business – for example, changes in the frequency/intensity of storm events, precipitation, carbon pricing and policy intervention levels in New Zealand and abroad. Meridian also developed scoring criteria with which to benchmark international and local scenarios. As a result of this review, Meridian concluded it could best access the necessary variables by sourcing data and information from a combination of: the Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS); IPCC Shared Socioeconomic Pathways; New Zealand – Climate Change Commission (CCC); and New Zealand – National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research (NIWA) Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) scenarios." Additionally: "Meridian is confident that its climate scenarios are relevant and appropriate for assessing the resilience of our business model and strategy to climate-related risks and opportunities... The international and domestic scenarios we have used to develop our own are, however, widely used by entities for conducting scenario analyses and climate risk assessments (both internationally and in New Zealand)."
answers.location_hint: p.12–13 (Relevance of scenarios and scenario methodology)
answers.notes: Justification is provided on two grounds: (1) the scenarios were selected after developing scoring criteria to benchmark which sources best provided the required variables for Meridian's unique business context; (2) the chosen scenarios are widely used internationally and in New Zealand for climate risk assessments.
